blurb: A non-stop bloomer — lavender-blue pincushion flowers float on wiry stems from late spring to frost, drawing butterflies and bees all season long to this compact, easy-care Front Range perennial.
water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
spread: Clumping; slowly expands but not aggressive
seasons: Mound of ferny gray-green foliage spring through fall; lavender-blue flowers May–Oct; dies back to ground in winter
wildlife: Outstanding butterfly and bee plant; a top nectar source for monarchs, swallowtails, and native bees

care.hardiness: Hardy to USDA zone 5 — reliable across the Front Range (zones 5b–6a). Excellent cold tolerance combined with heat tolerance makes it unusually well-suited to Colorado's wide temperature swings. The compact cultivar 'Butterfly Blue' (Perennial Plant of the Year 2000) and 'Pink Mist' are the best-proven Front Range selections. Intolerant of wet winter soil; sharp drainage is critical for winter survival.
care.planting: Plant in spring (Apr–May) or early fall (Sep) from nursery transplants. Spring is the most common approach; early fall planting works well in Boulder and lets roots establish before cold. Average, well-drained soil is all it needs — no special soil prep required. In containers, pincushion flower performs well as a seasonal display plant; overwinter pots in a protected spot or move them to an unheated garage, as exposed roots in a pot are far less cold-hardy than in-ground plants.
care.sun: Full sun is ideal for maximum bloom — 6+ hours. It tolerates light afternoon shade, which can actually help prolong flowering during Boulder's hottest July stretches. Avoid deep shade, which reduces blooming dramatically.
care.soil: Average, well-drained soil with a neutral pH suits it perfectly — Colorado's alkaline loam is a fine match. Good drainage is essential; it will not tolerate waterlogged or consistently wet soil, especially in winter, which causes crown rot. Lean to moderately fertile soil produces the best flowering; overly rich soil grows lush foliage at the expense of blooms.
care.water: Low to moderate. Water regularly through the first growing season to establish deep roots; afterward, water deeply every 1–2 weeks in summer heat. Once established it is fairly drought-tolerant, but consistent moisture through the bloom season keeps flowering going. Avoid overhead watering and ensure no standing water around the crown in winter.
care.spacing: Space plants 12–15 in apart — they form compact mounds that don't need much room. Works well at the front of a border or in a rock garden.
care.propagation: Easiest from division in spring every 2–3 years, which also keeps the clump vigorous and free-flowering. Named cultivars like 'Butterfly Blue' are vegetatively propagated; the straight species can be grown from seed started indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost (mid-May around Boulder) or direct-sown in spring. Seeds germinate in 10–15 days at ~65–70°F.
care.bloom: Late spring through frost — typically May into October on the Front Range, with a peak in June–July and again in September. This is one of the longest bloom seasons of any Front Range perennial. Regular deadheading is key to maintaining the continuous display.
care.feeding: Light feeding. A single application of a balanced slow-release fertilizer in spring is sufficient. Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers, which push foliage over flowers. Plants in average soil often need no feeding at all.
care.maintenance: Deadhead spent flowers regularly by clipping stems down to the basal foliage — this is the most important task for keeping bloom continuous all season. Cut back any remaining flowering stems to the basal rosette in fall after frost; clean up dead foliage in early spring before new growth emerges. Divide every 2–3 years in spring to maintain vigor and prevent the center from dying out.
care.troubles: No serious pest or disease problems on the Front Range. Watch for aphids on new growth and occasional powdery mildew in humid or crowded conditions — good airflow and sharp drainage prevent both. Root rot can occur in poorly drained or wet winter soil; ensure drainage is adequate. Short-lived perennial if drainage is not maintained.
